with five or six cardboard boxes on a floor, with different topics written on the side in a marker, and throwing shit into the boxes as I got it. And then I'll go take all of my old stuff that could be recycled, which is really a Carla project, that's go back through all the newsletters and find anything that even closely has anything to do with selling to the affluent. I'll pull it out for me, put it on one disc, print it out. Take all the marketing letters, go back through the old books, which I don't do, I have her do, and find anything, throw in one of these boxes. And then ultimately I sit down and write from the boxes. I actually did, I actually did the time management book all in one, and I did it on a Saturday, a Sunday, and by noon on Monday. But did you have like you had most of it? Sure. Organized or in your head? Yeah, well some of it in the head, but some of it, you know, so I had little piles. So how about the other books though? If you don't write them in one chunk, what kind of little chunks? I write for, I try, I'm not as perfect at it as I used to be, but I try for my, what I call my stuff, my books, products, in other words, not direct response copywriting for clients, and not Glaser Kennedy stuff. I try and write an hour each morning. Now I used to be like, militant, you know, so like, this morning I did, but yesterday morning I was too bagged, I didn't do it. But probably, I don't know, 250 mornings a year I do it, and I accumulate pieces. And I don't even really try, so now for the affluent book, there's also a file on the computer where I have been writing hunks. And they may have, they have little or nothing, they're not connected, it's not like I sat down and wrote a chapter, but there's probably 100 pages of stuff there that is accumulated. Now in pre-computer, of course I did all that on typewriters, I would cut the stuff apart with a scissors and put it in the, now you actually can put page breaks in and print it out, but so I do hunks. And then ultimately I have enough stuff to be the book, right? And I'm a too draft guy, so I will just write it, then again I'll go back with my checklist and try and figure out what I got right, what I don't have right, how to rearrange the order, oh shit there's no story that does this. Now I gotta go find something that does this, and that'll be my second draft and that's it. If that you can turn the box and turn it up, do you can write about 50 pages a day in other words if you have to? If I have to? Yeah. I mean you were talking about doing a book in four days, I assume that's about 50 pages? Yeah maybe even a little more. I did new psychosobernetics which of course was a rewrite of psychosobernetics, so that makes life easy, I did that in a day. In a day? In typewriter. Two. What do you say? Now you can fudge subheads and activity boxes and yeah. I mean, I'm just trying to comment. I'm assuming Michael's asking the question, and maybe John as well, to give yourself sort of a blueprint for what you can aspire to accomplish. But I think it's like anything else, that the fact that Danny can knock out an OBS marking letter in four or five hours, or a book in four days, has a lot to do with the fact that he does a lot of writing. And that makes you much, much more efficient. I mean, am I PGS marking letter which I've been doing now for nine years? I mean, it takes me approximately a third of the time, it used to take me nine years ago to do it, but a lot of that has to do with the fact that I do a lot of writing. And so, this is like the caution on the side of the cigarette pack is, don't try this at home. Well, and I put some notes, I didn't reference them, but they're in your notebook. A couple pages out of the Stephen King book, which I gave you the whole book, skip the first half of the book if you want to, because that's what my childhood was and all that shit, but the last half is all about writing. And I put some other notes in, you'll find a notebook. And very much any writer will tell you, you write. That's, if you're going to be a writer, you write. And see, even the, so it's doing a lot of it, it's doing a lot of it within narrow confines, right? So like the fiction guys who do serial fiction, rather than, you know, like Stephen King starts from scratch every time with different characters, right? And the guys who are doing, you know, Clancy when he's still road, which he doesn't, he has a factory now, but Clancy or, you know, those kind of people, they have a much easier time, because typically they got only three or four story structures. They have some kind of a checklist like this of here's what has to happen, right? They have a structure they write to every time, so they can be a lot faster. They write a lot, which helps. And almost every one of them has some version of the hour every morning. I mean, they have a ritualistic routine, like King writes every day for half a day. Christmas, New Year's, birthday, to my any day. And he's got a page quota to hit. Now it can be shitty, but we're going to hit that page quota and worry about quality later. Well, that's like, who are we talking about? Oh, you, with your wife doing the sleep coach, right? And, you know, the key for people that have trouble sleeping, the whole key is the ritual. You got to go in at exactly the same time. You got to do the same stuff in the same way. Writing is the same way. See, because the subconscious gets it when it's trained by place, time, routine, and discipline. So, okay, we show up at the same place at the same time every day. We do the same things to get ready to write, boop, we're in gear. So, the person who only writes three, four times a month in order to get his newsletter out, or in order to do two products a year, he's severely handicapped because he has like, I've never had writer's block. I don't know what that is, right? But it's because the subconscious doesn't get, it's trained to do it. Usually, it's started before the fingers have on the keys. So, the speed, and of course, the last thing about being willing to churn it out is willing to sacrifice, you know, quality. I mean, there's no doubt you could take anything I do, not your editor, but I mean, a good editor who wasn't really getting in the way, but was trying to help it. There's no doubt you could improve everything I do. I mean, it's just, it's got to be done by X time, and it's as good as it's going to get in X amount of time, and then it's got to move. I mean, I'll spend a lot more time worrying about a sales letter than I will this. Not that this isn't important, but there's no direct, oh shit, we're going to mail 50,000 of these things, and it better bring in X amount of dollars, or you know, the nooses around your neck. But bottom line is the key to it is if you're going to be a writer, which I would suggest you need to define yourself as, because really the big tool of our businesses is speaking, if you're going to go do that, and writing. Those are the big tools of the business, and that really is the businesses we're in. We just don't usually describe it as such, but if you're going to be a writer, you've got to write. That's bottom line. And the fact that you write speeds you up and makes you more proficient, makes you less dependent on checklists, because now you're referencing them all the time in your head, whereas if you're referencing them once a month, they never like get planted in there for automatic access. Well, I don't know, half my life between copywriting and this kind of writing, half my life. I gave you two sample structures in your notebook. You could live off the two. There's about 20. It's all there are. And a few books on writing, I mean, you go get, pick your choice of any writer's digest book on how to write a novel, and you're going to find four or five of the structures. It is useful to write the structure. Now, some people like to write to outline. I don't do that. Less writers do it than do it. But the main thing is you got to do it. And you got to kind of decide, as always, what are the highest value things in my business to do, and how can I get rid of something else so I can focus on this, you know? And I mean, my speed has accelerated even just in the recent years because of him. So I've offloaded things that before I had to give time to and I had to pay attention to. Some of which maybe I'm as capable as Bill, but don't like doing other things. Like the movie. Yeah.
